title: Marxism and Literary Criticism
excerpt: A concise and lucid introduction to Marxist literary criticism, covering the relationship between literature and social history, the question of authorial commitment, and the debates between Marxist critics and formalists.
keyIdeas: Literature is part of the superstructure determined by the economic base
Literary form is ideological — shaped by and shaping the social relations of production
The writer is both a producer and a product of history
The relationship between base and superstructure is mediated, not mechanical
Marxist criticism must attend to the specific formal properties of literary works
keyTakeaways: Marxist criticism analyzes the relationship between literary texts and their material historical conditions
Literature and ideology are mutually constitutive — literature can both reinforce and challenge dominant ideologies
The concept of 'authorial commitment' is more complex than simply writing political propaganda
Marxist and formalist approaches are not necessarily incompatible
whoShouldRead: Students of literary theory seeking an introduction to Marxist criticism
Scholars interested in the relationship between literature and politics
Readers of Eagleton's Literary Theory who want to explore his Marxist framework in depth
whoShouldSkip: Those already well-versed in Marxist literary theory — this is an introductory text
Readers resistant to any form of politically engaged criticism
